Hi. Today we're sitting down with Bond and Kevin Halbert, the second generation of one of the greatest copy and marketing dynasties the world has ever seen. To talk a little bit about halbertology. Bond, Kevin, thank you so much for meeting with us today. I am sure that all of the fans of halbertology are going to be really excited to hear about everything that you have to tell us. Well, thanks for having us. Thank you. So let's get started, shall we? Can you tell me how being in business for yourselves in copywriting and marketing has transformed your lives? I tried working nine to five, and I just can't do it. Going to the same place and seeing, you know, find seeing the same people, but going to the same place and seeing the same four walls and doing the same thing over and over again gets really tedious on me. And so it really just didn't work out very well. So one of the things that doing this on our own and taking control of your own destiny has allowed me to do is, you know, it gives you a freedom that a lot of people talk about, but I'm kind of like a living example of it. My kids have not really spent a lot of time with babysitters. I have never missed their plays. My retired mom wants to go out to lunch. I'm free to do so. Every single, you know, I mean, if I see a movie I want to go see, I go and check it out on Monday morning. So, you know, the freedom of controlling the schedule and being able to do what I want means the world to me. Yeah. And I like the idea that I get to work at night. I like that I get to work at night and I get to choose, you know, where I gotta, you know, go. I always go sit in a coffee house and work because it gets me into a mode and I just get to sit there. And so if, so I choose my coffee houses by how late they're open. And then that's, that's my commute. If I decide to go to a coffee house, if I want to work at home or whatever, and then, you know, we get to trade off our time. We get to trade off, go, do we want more money? Do we want to put more time? Or do we want to relax more and just, you know, enjoy? When you're working nine to five, you can't just take off to go spend time with loved ones who are in the hospital. Or you can't just, you know, say, yes, I'll be there no matter what to people who want, you know, to have to be with you. So the freedom for me has meant an incredible amount. And then if I have, you know, volleyball coming up, I can go, I want to go play volleyball today. I'm gonna have to do that during the day. So I'll just go do that. Yeah, we're two different animals. It's easy to see how you were confident that you would succeed, but not everyone has the Prince of Print for a dad. Do you think anyone can do what you do and why? I certainly do. I've seen a bunch of people do it. I mean, you know, one of the things, our father was an extremely pedantic guy. He just loved to teach. He loved to teach his craft. He touted to us. I mean, and as soon as he'd learned something, he wanted to teach us. And so we were with him and paying very close attention when he, you know, I always tell people he quit his last job that was fired the day I was born or the day before I was born. So we grew up with him always being an independent, self-made person. But we were very cognizant and very much aware when he started teaching everybody else. In fact, he really, his whole guru status kind of boils back down to his birthday. And when he sat down and wrote the very first boron letters, which start, you know, which was his plan, starting off and writing his newsletter and then teaching his craft to other people. And so by that time, he was already teaching Kevin and I the business. And so we sat around and watched him not only train tons and tons of people, but the guys who are everybody is saying, you know, they're the biggest and the best in the business of things. We watched him train them. And we watched also, you know, it's not like a one man, you know, it's not just a one way street. Our father would learn from them the same way we learn from other copywriters and marketers that we work with. But you know, if there's anything I'm 100% sure of is that anybody can do it because I've watched a whole bunch of people come in very green. And then, you know, with time, now here's the thing, they all started. And that's a key thing. They started and then what they were doing. And they kept at it. And those that started and kept at it really did do it. And there was no, you know, there was no defining well, that guy's got this star quality and he's going to make it in this business. There it was, if they put in the time, they put in the work, they put in the effort, they did it. Yeah, I think that the fact that we have all the success stories from people that have just learned from what he's left behind that were, you know, the seminars and stuff like that. There's all these massive success stories from people just who never met him, never knew him, and never had to be around them the extra time that we did. Of course, we got something more out of being around him. But, you know, the thing is, is that he's concepts and everything he teaches, you get it, you know, all of his stuff, I still go through to this day over and over again. And I get something deeper out of the same thing I already know, because I went through it again, and it keeps going like that. Some of the guys went on to become hired guns, highest paid copywriters around. Other guys put their copy only to you selling their own wares, you know, whereas, and other people took the lessons and understood the key concepts behind them were a lot greater than just copywriting. Anyone can do it. I mean, he wrote the Warren letter so that, you know, if he wasn't there, someone could learn to do it just from the letters that he wrote. Marketing is anything to do with human relations when you want another human to do something. So, if you're trying to get your kid to do your homework, it's marketing. Ada, attention, interest, desire, and action. If you learn the core principles behind every single strategy and technique, then you just have a lot of power that goes far beyond just copywriting. And it's, of course, useful in every aspect of marketing. He was teaching us, you know, Kevin and I, what I was like, maybe between 9 and 11, and, you know, Kevin's 18 months older than I am. So, we've been learning for a long, you know, we were learning a long time even before that. I always, it's classic, I always say thank God, Dad went to prison because if it weren't for that, I wouldn't have the proof that we had this incredible education at an early age because you think, okay, well, his dad's a neurosurgeon, doesn't mean anything that he's got skills, you know, but it was different in our case. Like I said, our father just loved to teach, and he loved to teach us. What's our regular day like for you? Well, one of the things I know it's about other writers and copywriters and marketers is sometimes you have a ritual of getting ready. You know, you do these things, you get your coffee, you put it on your table, you do whatever, because it lets your mind get in that mental mode to start working. But when I get into the emails, one of the things is I like to make sure that anybody writes us or does anything that I don't take care of them in the afternoon. If they had a problem overnight, I want them taking care right away. And what I'm doing is, is I'm freeing myself for the rest of the day. I don't have to wonder if there's something I, somebody's got a problem, I need to take care of it. And once I get check my emails, fix everybody's things, if there is anything, then I'm, then I'm into my routine of doing what I'm going to do for the rest of the day. And that's just going, what promotion do we want to work on? What the biggest step that's in the way, or what do I have to get going that has to be going while I'm getting the other pieces all set up. And so we usually, he rings me when he can finally ring me 10am, and then we make our game plan, we go, what are we trying to accomplish? What are the things we need to do? What do you, what can you do? What can I do? And what we'll get it, you know, done. And so we just kind of attack it that way. And it's usually a no brainer, because we just figure out, Oh, you've got the skill set for that, you've got the skill set for that. And then so we know, we know what we're expecting about each other when we come back around and go, did the thing get done? What's in the way? How do we take that? And then we just, every once in a while, check in to make sure that we're still on the same page. And know each other, where each other is on the tasks we're trying to accomplish. I'm the exact opposite. I actually do all my creative work in the beginning of the day. I get up early. I usually wake up between five and five 30am and without an alarm clock. I don't even, I don't honestly know if I own an alarm clock. What I find most copywriters and marketers and creatives do is they find, they carve out that time that's quiet time for them. Okay, for me, that happens to be in the morning, because my brother's asleep, my wife and kids have gone off, you know, to do their lives and so forth. And the other thing that's in common with almost all copywriters and marketers is that routine he's talking about. It's critical because it's the only way that you can rely on exactly, this is when my brain's going to be hitting on off cylinders. Because if you know, you quickly learn when you have to perform creatively, when you have to come up with a good idea, you have to be responsive. You very, very quickly learn that, you know, we as humans are so, such products of our environment. In fact, Kevin mentioned something that's kind of like a key hack that you could, which just shows and proves this. And my dad taught me this too. He said, if you're ever had in a funk and you just can't get into the mood to work, go to a research library. We're such social creatures, it's impossible to be in a research library with a bunch of people working and not all of a sudden get into the mode to work. He goes to a coffee house where there's a ton of people reading and doing homework and writing reports and, you know, scripts for their plays and stuff like that. So what happens is he automatically will get, you know, into the vibe and the mood to work. When you go beyond that, you start to realize, okay, if I have a little too much coffee, it changes the way I think. You know, you start to really learn that your brain is a muscle that needs input, it needs nurture, it needs rest, it needs creative, you know, input, it needs all kinds of stuff. And so the, all the copy, every single top copywriter I know, and even every single creative top marketer I know, the people who hire the copywriters, they get into a routine that nails it down so that they can be sure that they're going to be on fire. I mean, I, if you want to make sure that you have me in my prime, it's between Monday and Thursday and it's between, you know, eight o'clock and ten o'clock in the morning, that's prime time. And if you catch me at that time, it's better for, you know, I get, sometimes people would ask me for an interview and I would say this is the time that I prefer and so forth because I know that I'm going to be on. I also know that, I mean, I'm so aware of my schedule. I know when, I know when the office is going to be too no easy, you know, I know because, because that affects filming. I know, you know, what not to eat the night before and I know that I need a certain amount, if I don't get out and exercise and get enough outside airtime and sunlight on my face and all this stuff. So it comes down to a very boiled routine, boiled down routine. Many of the greatest names in copywriting and marketing have come together in honor of your dad. What does having this lineup mean to you? Part of it to me was, well, I mean, if you're going to be really honest about it, it was okay, what can we put together? What would these people want? We did a survey and we said, what can we deliver them? We had already done the Gary Halbert Letter All Star audio series. We had that mistake of the audio series where we invited a bunch of people expecting half of them to flake and none of them dropped out. And so we ended up making a double album on the first output and, you know, so we ended up with 12 people and then we did another album, you know, another seven, six, seven people and then we'll have the same accident kind of happen. We were like, who do we want on this project? Who can give these people what they want? When they're done going through it, who's going to give them stuff that will make their lives better and will actually have an impact and effect if they execute? And so we got into that dream stage and the same thing happened. We thought about inviting a few people and we invited a lot of people and they all said yes. And it got to the point where we felt very honored that they respected our dad that much, that it looked pretty much and it's turned out so far that everyone we asked said yes. And then it became, we can give these people anything they want because they're all on board. So, you know, so the question becomes, all right, we can get all these people, we can get these great people, but we can have anybody. And I don't mean that in a good way and what can we put together that will be the best for everybody, for all the audience. And the thing was part of this was a little selfish motivation. Part of the selfish motivation is we're kind of fans of these people and these guys are kind of big experts. And when it comes to the 2.0 era, these people who respect our fathers so much kind of further our education. Part of it was me getting what I want and knowing that what I want out of learning this, getting this group together to make this big giant thing, which wasn't really originally planned to be a big giant thing, is, you know, what can we put in here that will, that I want to see because I'm the same as the people we're going to provide all this stuff for that we got out of the survey. For me, the first initial reaction was I felt really blessed. I picked up the phone and started calling inviting people for the All Star Audio series and then one day all of a sudden I'm talking to big names in the business that everybody else is paying a lot of money to get access to and so forth. And they're picking up the phone and answering in honor of our father and what he's done, but what makes it even better for, I think, the audience that's really great about this and Kevin's alluding to it, is these are all Gary Halbert fans. And the one thing that Gary Halbert fans did is they take a, like I was talking about those core strategies and when they take it, they're applying this to the areas that we don't work in. They're, you know, they're applying it to, you know, podcasting and, you know, for traffic generation and YouTube channels and, you know, PPC and all of these different kinds of places. And the, there's two things about this. One is they're really well-named recognized experts and that's more than just star power. What that is is that security and knowing that you're learning what's right, you know. You know, the difference between just, you know, asking somebody, hey, what's a good way to write this or what's a good way to do this and hearing a whole, you know, like, how do I write a good short story? Well, when you're getting the answer from Stephen King, it's not just that Stephen King's a great writer, it's that you know he's a good writer. There's a lot, you could be in a room with a bunch of strangers and you're not sure which one's a good writer and they're all kind of giving you slightly adjusted advice. It brings a level of expertise that you can rely on, you know, and that's what, that's what comes with the, that's what branding is. Branding is when people know by the name that they know they're getting something that's of high quality and these are all high quality marketers. And so that's a big benefit and it's a big benefit to us. As Kevin's saying, we're going to be students here for a lot of this. You know, when it comes to the copywriting and it comes to a lot of the marketing, we're going to be in there giving answers and you know presenting and so forth. When it comes to some of the stuff these people are doing, we are going to be taking notes just like anybody else would. But the, the advantage that these guys are going to bring and show us is part of connection to the Gary Halbert teachings. Because when you have Gary Halbert teachings and you go into something like PPC, you get an automatic advantage. You get an automatic advantage in knowing how this is the image and these are the words that are going to go right to the core of what this person truly wants and desires and that emotional trigger that's going to make them buy, that's going to make them follow your call to action and do what it is you want to do. This is the one everybody's dying to know. What was it like growing up as a son of Gary Halbert? It was different. I can tell you that being Gary Halbert's son was not the normal experience. Imagine having a father whose brain can't figure out anything that's not related to persuading another human being. It can't drive down the street. It can't make you breakfast, literally. Okay, our father could not make us breakfast if we wanted. If mom went away, who was out of town and there wasn't a housekeeper to cook, we were eating stuff out of a liquor store type of thing. But he really was this guy whose brain just, it was like 95% of every gray cell in his brain was only working on persuasion and marketing and copywriting and there was nothing else to it. His insane focus is unparalleled. You could not do it. You couldn't be a Gary Halbert and you know and match that unless you, and I'm not willing to do it, unless you, there's a price to pay. Yeah, you have to give up caring about everything for, except for that for 30 years. The focus and dedication was so intense. It's almost like an affliction. Yeah, and then there was, then there's the aspects of his personality, which was my father was always messing with people. Just, you know, the only thing he did, if he was in a room with you and you're just hanging out, he still found it was compelling for him to try and push your buttons, try and make you happy, try and make you upset, try and motivate you to do something or whatever. And what he was doing was social experimentation. He was messing with people all the time, trying to figure out where these hot buttons were, what makes you react. Because a lot of people think copywriting is about fancy writing and creativity and being really good with words. It does help. There is some wordsmithing involved and there's some talent and our father had an incredible vocabulary that you wouldn't have recognized if you'd just read his copy. But it all boils down to human psychology. That's what you say was more important than how you said it. And he practiced that on us. So imagine, you know, every dinner conversation started with clickbait. You know, all of us, you know, every conversation starts off with something like, you know, hey, I just figured out a way to make 20 million dollars, you know, and he's waiting for us to go, really? How? And, you know, we, I used to resist and he said that was cruel. Yeah, I wouldn't take the bait. But the, but our father was doing that all the time. And so he did it with us. He, you know, he did it with his friends and everything. So we saw it constantly. And he, so there were times it was a little embarrassing to be growing up. Yeah. Well, until you got into the groove of it and you started to enjoy it like I did. And then all of a sudden, you know, I'm torturing Kevin as much as my dad is with embarrassment. And, but he, but the other thing was the way that he thought was a little bit different. It was a little bit more core. You know how a comedian will point out something that's just the ridiculousness of the situation? He was great at that. He was great at pointing out that ridiculousness and recognizing it and seeing it. But he was also great at problem solving and getting right to the heart of stuff. One thing about that I learned about, now it's not true of every single marketer, but it's true of a lot of savvy marketers is that savvy marketers that are good at copywriting develop a odd sense of humor. And I think that comes from when you're a marketer, you have to stop being politically correct and you have to start understanding true human nature. When you start understanding true human nature, you start to laugh at it all. That's one of the things. And once you do that, there's a certain thing where you kind of see what people say they are and what they really are. And then, then you start to play with it and start to mess with people. And one of the things that he used to do just to mess with people, because he just didn't care anymore, was the one time I remember I was standing with him, we got in this elevator, there was a guy and we were in Miami during the, when all the models were down there, you know, modeling, there's just concentration of models everywhere. But obviously, we got this elevator, there's a guy in the elevator, and very obviously a model. And the doors close and my dad turns around and goes, I just want to ask you, well, have you taken Jesus into your heart today? And this guy like backs up to the wall and the girl gets it. And she's starting to crouch down. She's laughing so hard, you think he's going to pee in the elevator. She's just, she's can't control herself from laughing. And that was his thing. He did, you know, total strangers. They were, they were not people in a sense. They were just guinea pigs for whatever. And it was not, and it wasn't just marketing, he couldn't turn off that thing. It didn't, he didn't have to have an experiment for something he was working on. He just had to double check on human nature and stuff. Okay, so the thing about them, the Mustang was is, in fact, I this subject came up once, when we were talking about, I think a survey actually, and you know, sometimes you think, Oh, maybe people won't tell you the truth and all this stuff. And it's like, when it comes to what people want, they will tell you exactly what they want, because they will be happy if you give that to them for what they want. So my dad had recently gotten out of a born federal prison camp, and he was, he was setting up his life again. I was living at my mom's house at the time. And he goes, Hey, Kevin, you know, what kind of car would you like? Well, I'd like a Ferrari Testarosa. That was the car of the day back then. And he's like, No, but within reason, I said, and I remember I was a commercial. So this commercial worked. It was funny. I was watching a commercial, there was a girl driving this white convertible Mustang in the hills in the 80s. And, and I said, a white convertible Mustang. And so he came back the following day and he says, How do you like my car? And I was like, and I went in the look, and it was a white convertible Mustang. And the thing that was interesting was, he asked me, because he doesn't care about cars, he makes, you know, he makes everybody else drive his cars. I mean, his Rolls Royce, we were driving it, you know, the Mustang, you name the car, we were always driving, he didn't care about cars, cars were not a status symbol for him in real reality. And so he knew at the end of the day, he could get me to do anything he wanted by telling me I could keep the car overnight, if I would go do the thing he wanted. So that would be drive him home, pick him up, get his drive, cleaning, do whatever. And so I didn't know, I thought he wanted to be a cool kid. I thought he wanted whatever the kids were like, because he wanted to be a younger guy and all that stuff. I had no idea I had handed him basically control of me hook, line, and sinker by doing a one little survey and going, What kind of car would you like? And he, he controlled me for what, like three years with that car. Because I just told I gave it up, I told him the secret. And I didn't dawn on me. And I think it wasn't until he passed away that I dealt. Oh, my God, that's what he was doing to me. The way that he would teach us, if we were going somewhere to learn, he'd say, you know, this is we're going to this meeting. This is what I'm going to do. This is what I want to pitch them. This is what I want. And we'll see how it will goes. And we'd be at, you know, in the meeting, right? And on the way home, he's like, Okay, did you see how so and so said this? This is what he's really going to do. And this is what this guy's thinking. You see where he said this? This is what he really meant. And this is where this comes from. So he'd break that all down and stuff like that. You know, but the only time he was really keeping the secret from us was when we were the guinea pigs. There was a really good time. What he was also good at. He also knew that being Machiavellian worked. And, you know, I think being Machiavellian to him was just what was the consequences of being that way. If it was worth the risk or it wasn't going to be a problem, he would do it. And I remember one time we were in LA. And it was back before they broke up the phone companies. There was just one phone company. And they didn't care because if they didn't treat you right, you weren't going to go anywhere else. So you had no choices. So it was back then it was like the DMV. You had to do it their way or else. And it was laid out like the DMV. The office was just like a whole bunch of tables in a massive room. And I remember because he got in a, it was the fancy double jacks for a fax machine. So you could take your phone line to use it both as a fax machine and telephone without having to plug them in. You can get them for 99 cents now. Back then this was a big deal. And I remember standing outside of this place. I even remember the location. And he goes, watch this. And he walks into this store, into this place. And he goes up to this woman. He says, you know, I'm coming here for the thing. And she goes, we don't have it. We don't have it. You can't have it. And he just, he just went ballistic. He just freaked out in this place and just made everybody in the room incredibly uncomfortable. Like he was livid, like he was fuming. And then when we walked out the door, he, his fuming disappeared. He turned into a smile and he goes, watch, they're going to have that for me when I come back in an hour. And he was not mad. He knew what it took to get it done. And so he just, and we walked back in an hour later and there was sitting on a desk the thing he wanted. And he knew what he had asked himself, which, you know, a lot of marketers should ask is, what do these people need to hear in order to buy? And, you know, can you do it ethically? But basically, what does it take? If you don't know that, if you don't have that answer, you can't give that answer, you can't do it. He always knew, he would always drill down on the truth. What does this take? What makes this event different? Howbertology, I mean, aside from the amazing guest roster, what makes it unique? I personally think what's going to stand out about it is usually you have a course that's giving you strategy in an area, one area or another. And you usually have about a, you know, a top branded, you know, well established expert who you know you can listen to. And then there's some filler in there, you know, to, you know, that's the information that you need to know, but you really could look up and find in a PDF online. But what we're putting together here is not only A-listers in each of these different segments and areas, people who can give you all the right strategies that you need to grow business right now, but they're doing it better than a lot of the other experts in their area. They're not just a best selling author. They're, you know, they got several New York Times best selling books on there. They're, you know, so they're not people who are just good at what they do or even greater what they do. They're some of the people who are basically the best at what they do. One of the interesting things is this will have stuff that interests people, but they're so broad. It's got so many experts in so many different fields, that there's going to be other stuff that maybe you didn't know of. And one of the interesting things is that one of the things that successful copywriters do once the second thing becomes successful is they stop being a copywriter. What they do is they hire other copywriters, but they have to be or they copy chief. And what that means is that they know what kind of mojo you have to have to write sizzling copy that will work. And so you can't just go, oh, you can hire copywriters. Go ahead and pay the ransom, a huge amount of money to get a copywriter when you don't know. But one of the things is that when you know how to do it yourself, then you know who to hire and how to do that. And you know what to look for and stuff like that. So when it comes to all these different experts, there's things that will come into these that you will never consider and are things that you will be looking out for. And it'll be things you'll be watching out for, for things that are pitfalls and stuff like that, that these experts will have. And so even if you don't do that yourself, you'll be able to either know how it really works, or, you know, how to, what the right questions to ask for the right person to do it, who are the right people to go to, what the right services to, you know, engage in all that stuff. So yeah, everything, there's no way that you're going to be doing all this stuff. But you might have a team that does all this stuff, or you might be wanting to engage in this stuff, or have add one of these components to what you're doing and scale up and do that. So, you know, it's, and I think the other thing is, is that what I think the keys to a program like this is being the right person that has the right understanding, core understanding of things, because that helps you write, ask these experts, the ones that we interviewed, but the ones that were having ones come in and just teach, is asking the right questions that don't waste the time, that make the most use of someone's precious time that is an expert that are coming in and giving us, and I've seen it before. I remember we did it with one person, and he was, he was on and we did an interview, and I asked him this question, he goes, oh, man, you're going to really pull this out of me. And you could tell that he was going to give me the answer, but he didn't want to give the answer, and, but I knew I asked the right question, and it was basically how to reverse engineer something he was doing. And so, he gave it up anyways, and he admitted, you know, this is how it works. And so, being in the right position to have the right and out of experience to ask the question that's really relevant and get those people to answer them, and, you know, and sometimes you'll find that we'll ask a question, you know, that maybe you didn't think of asking. Kevin, Bond, I really want to thank you for taking the time to sit down with me today. I'm sure everyone involved with Habritology is going to be really excited to hear everything you shared with us. Thanks so much.
